claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Firstly, could you please tell me how many children you're looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have one child – my son Alex, who is 18 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Since Alex is 18 years old, eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether he is currently in approved education or training.

Could you tell me if Alex is currently in full-time education or training? For example, is he studying for A-levels, a BTEC, or attending college?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He finished school and isn't studying anywhere right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Since Alex is 18 and not in approved education or training, I have a couple more questions to help determine your eligibility:

Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service (such as the National Careers Service) or the armed services since leaving education? This is relevant because Child Benefit can sometimes continue for up to 20 weeks for 16 or 17 year olds who register with these services after leaving education or training.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with anything like that. He just finished school and that's it.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

I have one more question to complete my assessment:

Is Alex currently working 24 hours or more per week, or receiving any benefits in his own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't work that much – he's not doing 24 hours a week or anything like that. And he's not receiving any benefits in his own name either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Unfortunately, based on what you've told me, I need to share some news that may be disappointing.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm sorry to say that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

**For Alex (age 18):**
- Child Benefit for children aged 16 or over normally only continues if they remain in approved education or training (until they turn 20).
- Since Alex has finished school and is not currently in any approved education or training, he does not meet this requirement.
- The exception that allows Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks when a young person registers with a careers service or the armed forces only applies to **16 or 17 year olds** – unfortunately, at 18, Alex doesn't qualify for this provision.

**What could change this:**
If Alex were to enrol in approved education or training (such as A-levels, a BTEC, NVQ up to Level 3, or similar qualifications), you may become eligible to claim Child Benefit for him again, provided he's under 20 when he starts.

I hope this information is helpful. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old. For children aged 16 or over, Child Benefit can normally only continue if the child stays in approved education or training (up until they turn 20). Alex is not currently in any approved education or training. Additionally, the provision allowing Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks when a young person registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services only applies to 16 or 17 year olds - since Alex is 18, this exception does not apply to him. Although Alex is not working 24+ hours per week and is not receiving benefits in his own name (which would disqualify him), these factors alone are not sufficient to establish eligibility - being in approved education or training is required for children aged 16 and over. Therefore, Alex does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The family has one child, Alex, who is 18 years old. Since Alex is over 16 and is not in approved education or training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit. The exception for registering with careers services or armed forces only applies to 16-17 year olds, so this does not help Alex's case at age 18.
